observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to –
• prepare and submit the PIP for approval of the Municipal Mayor to ensure
that the conduct of inventory is properly and adequately planned;
• submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least 10 days calendar days
before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities;
• ensure that property stickers specify vital information on the PPE items
and to adopt a uniform property identification system wherein a unique
Property Number shall be assigned for each PPE item;
• complete the conduct of physical count of all PPE at least once a year
and submit the RPCPPE classified per category/type to the Office of the
Auditor not later than January 31 of each year;
b) MGSO to update the PCs based on the result of the physical inventory and
maintain them per category of PPE with complete information about the PPE;
c) MAO to continue updating the subsidiary ledger cards to establish the details
of the remaining P13,447,745.47 PPE, using as reference the supporting
documents for their purchase or payment; and
d) MGSO and MAO to reconcile the balances of their respective records of PPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, the
Municipal Planning and Development Officer, the Municipal Accountant and other
concerned officials to propose fiscal policies, programs and specific,
measurable, achievable, realistic and timely action plan to resolve the persistent
problem on cash deficit of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to –
• refrain from processing the grant of additional cash advances to officers
and employees unless their previous cash advances have been accounted
and settled; and
• issue Demand Letters to all Accountable Officers with unliquidated cash
advances.
b) require all accountable officers to immediately liquidate their respective cash
advances as soon as the purposes for which the advances were granted has
been served by submitting the required Liquidation Reports and supporting
documents to the Accounting Office; and
Henceforth, we recommended that Municipal officials comply strictly with COA
Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
return to the Bureau of Treasury the balances of the fund transfers from various
NGAs that had been dormant for more than five years or the purpose of which
had already been achieved by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
record as inventories the goods procured for distribution/consumption upon
receipt and to recognize expense only when the goods are distributed/issued to
end-users or beneficiaries.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to exert effort to locate old files of other prepayments
account to facilitate adjustments in the books.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of all PPE at
least once a year, prepare and submit a copy of the RPCPPE to the
Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and coordinate closely
with the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) to reconcile the
RPCPPE with the accounting records;
b) MGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein relevant
information for proper monitoring; and
c) Municipal Accountant to maintain PPE Subsidiary Ledger Cards for each
group of PPE specifying the acquisition, description, custody, estimated
useful life, depreciation, impairment loss, disposal and other information
about the asset.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a) facilitate the filing of request for authority to write off the dormant and/or
uncollectible receivables totalling P2,747,844.33 to the COA, following the
guidelines and procedures provided by COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
b) exert effort to locate old files of other prepayments account to facilitate
adjustments in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal
Officials to institute additional cost-cutting measures and exercise diligence,
prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds to
address the incurrence by the Municipality of cash deficit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Municipal Accountant to take up in the books under the appropriate
inventory account the procurement or receipt of supplies and materials,
including welfare goods, and to recognize expense only when the goods
are distributed to end-users based on the RSMI submitted by the GSO, as
prescribed under IPSAS 12, Section 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume
I, and COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2015-09; and
b) Municipal General Services Officer-Designate/Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Officer to maintain Stock Cards to record the
purchases and issuances/distributions of supplies and materials. The
issuances of these items should be supported with the Requisition and
Issue Slip (RIS) and reported monthly to the Municipal Accounting Office
through the preparation and submission of the Report of Supplies and
Materials Issued (RSMI) as basis of the Municipal Accountant in recording
issued items under their respective expense accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant
to properly record the purchase and receipt of welfare goods for distribution
to various beneficiaries of the Municipality to Welfare Goods for Distribution
account and the corresponding issuance thereof to Welfare Goods Expense
account, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a) BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team justifications on its inability
to comply with the posting requirement under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020,
for further evaluation; and
b) BAC Secretariat and the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit
Team the Mayor’s or Business Permit, Income/Business Tax Returns and
Omnibus Sworn Statement (OSS) of the suppliers pertaining to COVID-19
expenditures totaling P3,514,370.33 to avoid issuance of audit suspension.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) OIC-MSWDO to submit justification to the COA Audit Team why three
Municipal government employees were included as ESP-SAP
beneficiaries when they were expressly excluded under Item VI-B of
DSWD MC. No. 09, series of 2020;
b) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the OIC-MSWDO, to submit to
the Audit Team the relevant certifications and/or photocopies of the
identification cards with specimen signatures of the identified 80
beneficiaries pursuant to DSWD MC No. 09, series of 2020, or valid
justification, for evaluation of the Audit Team, why the documents could
not be produced, otherwise the transactions will be suspended in audit;
c) Municipal Treasurer and OIC-MSWDO to investigate and explain the
discrepancies in the names or signatures in the payrolls, SAC, and valid
IDs of the 57 beneficiaries; and
d) Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to investigate and explain
why the financial assistance intended for five beneficiaries were
considered liquidated despite absence of signatures in the payrolls or
other proof that they received the benefits, otherwise refund the amount of
P32,500.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year 2020 to enable the latter Office to set the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year correctly.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to file a request with the Commission on Audit (COA) for the write-off of the dormant receivables totaling P2,800,137.09 and to locate supporting documents of the Other Prepayments amounting to P680,748.83, following the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

The Municipality will now file a request with COA for the write-off of the said dormant accounts. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant had exerted effort to locate old files of other prepayments to facilitate adjustments in the books.
observations.reason:
Non retrieval of old documents.
Reiterated in audit observation no. 2, part 2 of this report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Accountant and Treasurer to: a) immediately transfer the unexpended balance of DRRMF for CY 2019 and trust receipts in the General Fund to the Trust Fund to significantly lessen, if not totally eliminate, the cash deficit in the latter Fund and to give higher assurance that the amount would only be used for calamity prevention and response; b) adopt measures to ensure that the Municipality’s cash position is strictly monitored and the cash position is strictly managed to avoid cash deficit and ensure availability of cash when obligations become due; and c) ensure that all trust receipts are strictly spent only for the specific purpose for which they were created or which they were received.
observations.managementAction: The Treasurer’s Office has already transferred the unexpended balance of DRRMF for CY 2019 and trust receipts in the General Fund on June 3 and June 8, 2020, respectively, to the Trust Fund to significantly lessen, if not eliminate, the cash overdraft. The Municipality disbursed all funds considered as trust receipts for specific purposes for which they were created or received. The Municipality strictly monitored the cash position in the Trust Fund and eliminated its cash overdraft.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting further cash advances to personnel with outstanding cash advances and to institute measures to ensure that cash advances are liquidated on time. It is also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all payments to suppliers and other creditors of the Municipality are directly made by check except when there is satisfactory justification that it is highly impractical and impossible to make payments by checks. Also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Budget Officer to ensure that only expenditures directly related to disaster risk management are charged against the LDRRMF while administrative expenses are charged instead against the respective Municipal offices.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality strictly refrains from granting additional cash advances to personnel with outstanding cash advances. Salary deduction to employees with long outstanding cash advances was implemented. The Municipality set up a revolving fund for the immediate repairs and maintenance that are highly impractical and impossible to make payments by checks. The Municipality also disbursed all trust receipts only for specific purposes for which they were created or received.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts to locate old records and/or request from the BIR, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth Offices for certification/records of arrears or no-arrears for the years 2009 and earlier, to facilitate the remittance or adjustments in the books of the amount of P193,720.52.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments in the book of accounts have already been made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned officials to ensure that only expenditures directly related to disaster risk management are charged against the LDRRMF while administrative expenses are charged instead against the respective Municipal offices; and to ensure compliance with the provisions of JMC No. 2013-1.
observations.managementAction: Expenditures were intended for operation purposes to enhance the Municipality’s capabilities on Disaster Response. The Municipality had already resumed the implementation of the projects as soon as possible.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) Municipal Engineer to resume the implementation of the projects as soon as possible; and b) Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer to immediately inform the DSWD Regional Office No. IV-A of the lack of participants for the training programs and request for their replacement.
observations.managementAction: Of the amount P3,949,200.00, P672,350.00 was already utilized. The Municipality will immediately inform Regional Office No. IV-A of the lack of participants and request for their replacement.
observations.reason: Waiting for Regional Office replacement of participants.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with all the concerned officials, prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee an Inventory and Inspection Report of all the unserviceable property with complete information to facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal which ensure their immediate disposal to prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.
observations.managementAction: Out of 13 transportation vehicles, six were already disposed while various office equipment and furniture and fixtures were disposed through negotiated sale in July and September 2020.
observations.reason: Three of the transferred vehicles were still for coordination with the various agencies for its disposal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with all the concerned officials, review and re-establish the composition of the GFPS of the Municipality to conform with the requirements of PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has already re-established the composition of the GFPS to conform with the requirements of the JMC No. 2013-01.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to henceforth refrain from obligating and charging LDRRMF for expenditures which are not directly related to the implementation of disaster risk management activities and to strictly observe the guidelines set forth under NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1. Administrative expenses should be charged to the respective appropriations of the Office concerned funded out of the General Fund as provided under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality acknowledges the need for a supplemental LDRRMFIP. Reiterated in audit observation No. 6 that administrative expenses should be charged to the General Fund and not to LDRRMF.
observations.reason: Reiterated in audit observation No. 6.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly certified list showing the
names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year 2020 to enable
the latter Office to set up the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year
correctly.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant to file a request with the Commission on Audit
(COA) for the write-off of the dormant and/or uncollectible receivables totaling
P2,800,137.09 following the procedures provided under COA Circular No.
2016-005.

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant should exert effort to locate old files of
other prepayments account to facilitate adjustments in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Accountant and Treasurer
to:
a) immediately transfer the unexpended DRRMF for CY 2019 and trust receipts in
the General Fund totaling P3,111,698.28 to the Trust Fund to significantly
lessen, if not totally eliminate, the cash deficit in the latter Fund and to give
higher assurance that the amount would only be used for calamity prevention
and response;
b) adopt measures to ensure that the Municipality’s cash position is strictly
monitored and managed to avoid cash deficit and ensure availability of cash
when obligations become due; and
c) ensure that all trust receipts are spent only for the specific purpose for which
they were created or received.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting further cash advances to
personnel with outstanding cash advances and to institute measures to
ensure that cash advances are liquidated on time. It is also recommended that
the Municipal Accountant be instructed to send demand letters to officials and
employees with long outstanding cash advances emphasizing that failure to
settle the cash advances despite receipt of the demand letters shall constitute
a valid cause for withholding of their salaries.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all payments
to suppliers and other creditors of the Municipality are directly made to them
by check except when there is satisfactory justification that it is highly
impractical and impossible to make payments by checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
exert efforts to locate old Due to BIR, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth files and/or

certification of arrears or no-arrears, for the years 2009 and earlier, to facilitate
the remittance or adjustments in the books of the amount of P193,720.52.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget
Officer to ensure that only expenditures directly related to disaster risk
management are charged against the LDRRMF while administrative expenses
are charged instead against the General Fund appropriations of respective
Municipal offices.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Engineer to resume the implementation of the projects as soon
as possible; and
b) Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer to immediately inform
the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) Regional Office No.
IV-A of the issue on lack of participants for the training programs and request for
their replacement.

officials, review and re-establish the composition of the GFPS of the

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to continue their efforts in reconciling the noted variances to establish the reliability of the balances of the PPE accounts reflected in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a) the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to conduct a thorough review of the records to determine the validity of all reconciling items; b) the Municipal Treasurer to request from the bank copies of the Credit Memo and Debit Memos to determine their validity and effect the necessary adjustments in the books; and c) the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record in the books all valid reconciling items to present the actual cash account balance as of year-end.

The Municipality requested that the unreconciled balances be written-off to draw out the discrepancy as the same has been remitted per records of the Municipality. The Municipality requested that the unreconciled balances be written-off to draw out the discrepancy as the same has been remitted per records of the Municipality. The Municipal Accountant requested the Provincial Accountant on June 18, 2019 and received on July 2, 2019 to reconcile and verify the Due to and Due from accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a) the Market Administrator and Municipal Treasurer to strictly implement the relevant rules and regulations of the Mendez Market Code particularly on the imposition of fines and penalties to vendors or tenants with no updated Permits, along with the imposition surcharge for late or non-payment of fees and cancellation of lease right and/or eviction of delinquent stallholders; b) the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the preparation of the Contract of Lease of all the stallholders; and c) the Municipal Treasurer to update the records of receivables and payments by individual stallholders to determine their outstanding balances for monitoring and collection purposes and report the same to the Municipal Accountant for him to take them up in the books of accounts of the Municipality for fair presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality implemented the relevant rules and regulations of The Mendez Market Code particularly on the imposition of fines and penalties to vendors or tenants with no updated Permits, along with, the imposition surcharge for late or non-payment of fees and cancellation of lease right and/or eviction of delinquent stallholders; The Municipal Treasurer facilitated the preparation of the Contract of Lease in the East Building and Vegetable Sections. The Municipal Treasurer has already updated the records of receivables and payments of individual stallholders for monitoring and collection purposes.
observations.reason: No records the Municipality could not determine the claims of some stallholders. Hence, it was decided that as soon as the construction of the new Mendez Public Market have been completed and operational all existing contracts will be renewed to conform with the revised Market Code.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned Property officials to prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) of all the unserviceable property with complete information to facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the same, after which ensure their immediate disposal to prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.
observations.managementAction: The request for Sangguniang Bayan (SB) Resolution authorizing the disposal was already approved. Re-evaluation was conducted on the backhoe and bulldozer and based on the findings and recommendation, the same could still be repaired and put into use; hence it was decided not to continue with the disposal. The Municipal Accountant was instructed to prepare the necessary documents upon disposal.
observations.reason: Only the unserviceable motor vehicles will be disposed after evaluation of the Appraisal/Disposal Committee.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a) the Designated General Service Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to have all government vehicles owned by the Municipality marked with “For Official Use Only” under which should be written the name of the Municipality of Mendez to properly identify the vehicles as government property; and b) the Designated General Service Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to secure government plates for the 17 government motor vehicles.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed the concerned municipal official to take actions on the audit recommendations. With ongoing follow-up with the Land Transportation Office.
observations.reason: Some vehicles were still not marked with the Municipality’s name and logo.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a) the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to prepare a supplemental LDRRMF Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to incorporate, under a separate caption, the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended previous years’ LDRRMF; and b) the LDRRMO to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation of the utilization of the Fund.
observations.managementAction: Previous unexpended balances have already been incorporated in the LDRRMFIP. The Monthly Reports on the utilization of LDRRMF were already submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to, henceforth, limit the annual appropriation and disbursements for discretionary purposes to a maximum amount equivalent to two per cent of the actual receipts derived from the basic property tax in the next preceding year in compliance with the above-cited regulation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer had monitored the charging against the Extraordinary Miscellaneous Expenses so as not to exceed to the limit of two per cent of the actual receipt from the basic RPT of the next preceding year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to make representation with the contractors through the Engineering Department and require the posting of the prescribed amount of warranty security for the infrastructure projects that had been fully paid and to ensure that henceforth the posting of the required amount of warranty security be observed by all contractors prior to release of full payment for every completed/accepted infrastructure project to guarantee the performance of their responsibilities as prescribed in Section 62.2.3.3 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the contractors through the Engineering Department and required the posting of the prescribed amount of warranty security for the infrastructure projects that had been fully paid. Re-evaluation was done and additional warranty security was posted. The Municipal Accountant assured that warranty security will be required prior to release of full payment to contractors.

observations.recommendation: We strongly reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer, the Accountant and Treasurer to: a) strictly monitor and manage its cash position to avoid cash overdraft and ensure that cash would be available when obligations become due; b) intensify collection efforts in order to generate funds to cover the cash deficit and ensure that the unexpended DRRMF is backed-up with cash so that the Municipality’s readiness to respond in case of occurrence of calamities will not be affected; and c) ensure that inter-agency and other current liabilities that are considered as trust receipts be spent only for the specific purpose for which they were created or received.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality disbursed all inter-agency and other current liabilities that are considered as trust receipts for specific purposes for which they were created or received. However, cash overdraft still existed in the Trust Fund book amounting to P3,330,375.60. The trust receipts and the unexpended DRRMF for CY 2019 were later transferred to the Trust Fund book on June 3 and 8, 2020 as indicated elsewhere in the audit report.
observations.reason: Cash Overdraft still existed in the Trust Fund book amounting to P3,330,375.60. Reiterated in audit observation No. 3.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting further cash advances to personnel with outstanding cash advances and to institute measures to ensure that cash advances are liquidated on time. It is also recommended that the Municipal Accountant be instructed to send demand letters to officials and employees with long outstanding cash advances emphasizing that failure to settle the cash advances despite receipt of the demand letters shall constitute a valid cause for withholding of their salaries. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all payments to suppliers and other creditors of the Municipality are directly made to them by check except when there is satisfactory justification that it is highly impractical and impossible to make payments by checks.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were issued to accountable officers. The Municipal Mayor instructed the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to comply with the audit recommendations. Salary deduction will be implemented in the ensuing year. The Municipal Accountant was instructed to setup a revolving fund to be used for the repair and maintenance of motor vehicles including registrations of the same since some repair shops refuse to accept checks as payment.
observations.reason: Some purchases of supplies and repair and maintenance, and other operating expenses were still made through cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the full utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund in accordance with their Annual Investment Plan (AIP) so that desired socio-economic and environmental outcomes for which the fund was established are attained.
observations.managementAction: Out of the current appropriation for 20 per cent Development Fund for CY 2019, the Municipality implemented P32,862,987.15 total appropriation for 20 per cent Development Fund for CY 2019. The Municipality indicated that the rehabilitation of public market was not implemented due to anticipated release of loan. The purchase of lots for different purposes are still under negotiation.
observations.reason: The rehabilitation of public market was not implemented due to anticipated release of loan. The purchase of lots for different purposes are still under negotiation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to:
1. strictly monitor and manage its cash position to avoid cash overdraft and ensure that cash would be available when obligations become due;
2. intensify collection efforts in order to generate funds to cover the cash deficit and ensure that the unexpended DRRMF is backed-up with cash so that Municipality’s readiness to respond in case of occurrence of calamities will not be affected; and
3. ensure that inter-agency and other current liabilities that are considered as trust receipts be spent only for the specific purpose for which they were created or received.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant reasoned that after the transfer of unexpended DRRMF and other trust collections the anticipated receipt of Trust Fund’s Due from Other Funds the overdraft amounted only P48,845.97. Continuous effort is being done by the concerned government officials in finding ways to increase its annual collections. Like-wise, he will see to it that the transfer of Local DRRMF will be made before the end of the year.
observations.reason:
There was a noted increase in the cash overdraft affecting the transfer amounting to P72,681.25 after considering the anticipated receipt of Trust Fund’s Due from Other Funds.
Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 12, Page No.54

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to stop processing grants of cash advances to those personnel with outstanding cash advances and send out demand letters to those who have outstanding balances way beyond the allowable period. Non-settlement despite receipt of demand letters shall be dealt with accordingly by way of withholding of their salaries.
observations.reason:
Grant of cash advances was not strictly monitored and delay of receipt of liquidation by the accounting office.
Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 13, Page No. 56

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to and Municipal Treasurer to continue their efforts in reconciling the
noted variances to establish the reliability of the balances of the PPE accounts
reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to file
a request with the Commission on Audit (COA) for the write-off of the dormant
and/or uncollectible receivables totaling P2,800,137.09 following the procedures
provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a) The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to conduct a thorough
review of records to determine the validity of all reconciling items;
b) The Municipal Treasurer to request from the bank copies of the Credit Memo
and Debit Memos to determine their validity and effect the necessary
adjustments in the books; and
c) The Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record
in the books all valid reconciling items to present the actual cash account
balance as of year-end. Proposed Journal Entries are shown in Appendix 6.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Provincial
Accountant to verify the discrepancy in the balances of the accounts Due to and
Due from LGU of the Municipality with that of its reciprocal accounts in the books
of the Province and make the necessary adjustments.

We recommended further, the Municipal Accountant request the Provincial
Accountant to furnish him with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers showing the
amount collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve as basis in
recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality as provided for under
Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a. the Market Administrator and Municipal Treasurer to strictly implement the
relevant rules and regulations of The Mendez Market Code and its Revenue
Code on the imposition of fines and penalties to vendors or tenants with no
updated Business Permits, along with, the imposition surcharge for late or
non-payment of fees and cancellation of lease right and/or eviction of
delinquent stallholders;
b. the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the preparation of the Contract of Lease of
all stallholders; and
c. the Municipal Treasurer to update the records of receivables and payments by
individual stallholders to determine their outstanding balances for monitoring
and collection purposes and report the same to the Municipal Accountant for
him to take them up in the books of accounts of the Municipality for fair
presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of the
unserviceable properties in accordance with the policies and procedures
provided in COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992 or the Manual on
Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments by complying
with the requirements being impose by the Office of the Sangguniang Bayan;
and
b. The Municipal Accountant upon disposal to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to
drop from the book of accounts of the Municipality the disposed unserviceable
properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The Designated, General Service Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to have
all government vehicles owned by the Municipality marked with “For Official
Use Only” under which should be written the name of the Municipality of
Mendez to properly identify the vehicles as government property; and
b. The Designated, General Service Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to secure
government plates for the 17 government motor vehicles.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to identify and
incorporate, under a separate caption, in the LDRRMFIP the projects and
activities to be charged out of the unexpended previous years’ LDRRMF
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
b. The LDRRMO be required to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and
Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund to facilitate
proper monitoring and evaluation of the utilization of Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to
henceforth, refrain from obligating and charging LDRRMF for expenditures which
are not directly related to the implementation of disaster risk management
activities and instead strictly comply with the guidelines set forth under NDRRMC,
DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1. Administrative expenses
should be charge to the respective appropriations of the Office concerned funded
out of the General Fund as provided under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Budget Officer to, henceforth, limit the annual appropriation and
disbursements for discretionary purposes to a maximum amount equivalent to two
per cent of the actual receipts derived from the basic property tax in the next
preceding calendar year in compliance with the above-cited regulation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
make representation with the concerned contractors and require the posting of the
prescribed amount of warranty security for the infrastructure projects that had
been fully paid. Henceforth, strictly observe the posting of the required amount of
warranty security by the contractors prior to the release of full payment for every
completed/accepted infrastructure project to guarantee the performance of their
responsibilities as prescribed in Section 62.2.3.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We strongly reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to:

a. strictly monitor and manage its cash position to avoid cash overdraft and
ensure that cash would be available when obligations become due;
b. intensify collection efforts in order to generate funds to cover the cash deficit
and ensure that the unexpended DRRMF is backed-up with cash so that
Municipality’s readiness to respond in case of occurrence of calamities will not
be affected; and
c. ensure that inter-agency and other current liabilities that are considered as
trust receipts be spent only for the specific purpose for which they were
created or received.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to stop processing grants of cash advances to those personnel with
outstanding cash advances and send out demand letters to those who have
outstanding balances way beyond the allowable period. Non-settlement despite
receipt of demand letters shall be dealt with accordingly by way of withholding of
their salaries.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that, henceforth, the
provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and Section 93 of PD 1445 are strictly
observed by all concerned Municipal Officials. Payments for purchases of
supplies, repair and maintenance, donation and other operating expenses, be
made directly to the suppliers and the Municipal Accountant to deduct/withhold
the mandatory taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the full utilization of the 20%
Development Fund in accordance with the timeline indicated in their Annual
Investment Plan (AIP) so that desired socio-economic and environmental
outcomes are attained and immediate benefits therefrom are enjoyed by the target
constituents.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer were continuously their efforts in reconciling their respective records to establish the reliability of the balances of the PPE accounts reflected in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Revert the Accounts Payable amounting to P8,744.48 which had been outstanding for more than two years and against which no actual claims has been filed to the unappropriated surplus; and
2. Deposit to the Bureau of Treasury the amount of P2,400.00 representing the unexpended balance of trust liability in conformity with COA Circular Nos. Nos. 76-45 and 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant reverted the accounts payable to unappropriated surplus through a JEV dated June 18, 2018. The account of P2,400.00 was deposited to the account of the Bureau of the Treasury.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the requirement under Section 4.2 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 is fully met before making any advance payment for projects being implemented by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: No payment for mobilization was processed and paid in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that pertinent provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR are strictly adhered to and see to it that the pre and post qualification process are properly conducted.
observations.managementAction: The checklist for completeness of bidding documents was revised. No adverse observation was noted in the post audit of bidding transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor effect the voluntary settlement of the above cited expenditures that are disallowable in audit prior to the receipt of the Notice of Disallowance. Likewise we recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Mayor and the Local School Board ensure that only allowable expenses as provided under DEPED-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. 2017 are charged against the SEF.
observations.managementAction: The amount of P100,000.00 erroneously charged to SEF was transferred back to SEF from the Trust Fund established for the purpose.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor strengthen the existing GDFPS by setting-up its structure/composition (i.e. Execom; TWG; Secretariat) and defining their roles and responsibilities as provided under Section 4.1.A of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-1. Partially Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Accountant to ensure that only expenditures/projects enumerated under the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1 are considered in the charging against the 20% Development Fund and strictly utilized the fund in accordance with the guidelines set forth in the JMC to fully achieve the intention and purpose for which it was established.
observations.managementAction: The 20% DF were used as Municipality’s counterpart in implementation of Municipality’s road-network projects. AIP prepared in 2017 intended for 2018 showed projects and programs for implementation of capital in nature.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Section 22 of Revenue Regulation No. 4-2007 issued by the Bureau of Internal Revenue be adopted in the preparation of Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) to the advantage of the Municipal Government.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has adopted the 5% VAT in the preparation of ABC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairman to ensure that: a. a list of the Municipality’s submitted PPAs for said programs/projects and activities, inclusive of those awarded for implementation of infrastructure projects be submitted to the Audit Team at the beginning of the year; b. Posting of information in the publicized bulletin board be posted in signboards or black/bulletin boards following the provisions of the cited Circular; c. the Audit Team be informed that the appropriate project signboards and/or notices have been posted within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity; and d. maintain updated Project Status report on the publicized projects undertaken based on the data sourced from the monthly monitoring report prepared by Municipality.
observations.managementAction: In the preparation of ABC some items of expenditures were not included, hence, project cost were maintained within reasonable level. Posting of information in the publicized projects undertaken. The Submission of PPAs for projects was submitted. Posting of project signboards and/or notices were posted in signboards. Submitted quarterly publicized projects undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Development Council to observe proper planning in the preparation of the Annual Investment Plan by providing itemized appropriation for every specific development projects/activities in conformity with Section 454(d) of the IRR of RA No. 7160.  We also recommended strict adherence to Section 454(d) of the IRR of RA No. 7160 which require itemized appropriation for specific development projects/activities embodied in the local development plan and  Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG) and Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 on the programs and projects to be funded from the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2016 expenses which were not capital in nature were still charged to 20% DF
observations.reason:
Management reasoned out that such expenses were part of their social development program.

Some projects were implemented depending on the request of the constituents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor strengthen the existing GDFPS by setting-up its structure/composition (i.e. Execom; TWG; Secretariat) as provided under Section 4.1.A of JMC No. 2013-1 and clearly define the roles and responsibilities of  GDFPS members  guided by the provisions of the JMC.

Likewise we recommended that the GAD database, through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator Office, be completed/ maintained/ updated,  taking into consideration the required data per PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction:
The structure of Municipality’s GFPS was not considered in the reorganization of the Municipality’s GFPS.

No database was establish
observations.reason:
The structure/ composition of the Municipality’s GFPS was overlooked in the reorganization of the same

Data and information available was not updated and consolidated due to retirement of the MPDO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that  the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant to:
exert extra effort in retrieving documents/information or records that will support and facilitate the computation of the depreciation expenses for   PPE not subjected to depreciation.     However, if effort prove to be futile, create an appraisal committee that will determine the fair value of these assets and the remaining useful life to serve as basis in the computation of depreciation on the aforesaid  property for fair presentation  in the financial statements; and
keep and maintain updated Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) for each class of PPE  for reference in the computation of depreciation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to reconcile their respective records and make the necessary adjustment to come up with reliable account balances and for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor demand for the immediate/full liquidation of all outstanding cash advances.    We  also recommended that he direct the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that no additional cash advances are granted to officials/ employees with outstanding/unliquidated balances and refrain from granting cash advances to elected officials except for their official travelling expenses;
reclassify the recorded Advances to Officers and Employees totaling P177,638.49 to Other Receivables for accurate  presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements by taking up the following adjusting entry:
Other Receivables                                    177,638.49
Advances to Officers and Employees                           177,638.49
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor request from  the Commission on Audit for authority to write-off the long outstanding cash advances following the guidelines and procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances and fund transfers set forth under  COA Circular  No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to verify the discrepancy in the Due to and Due from LGU accounts of the Municipality with that of its reciprocal accounts in the books of the Province and make the necessary adjustments; and
request the Provincial Accountant to furnish him with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers showing the amount collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve as  basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality as provided under Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  advise the   Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to:
strictly monitor and manage its cash position to avoid cash overdraft and ensure that cash would be available when obligations become due;
find ways to cover the cash deficit with the collections in the ensuing year to ensure that the unexpended 20% Development Fund and LDRRMF are backed-up with cash so that programs and projects under these funds could be sustained; and
exercise prudence in the disbursement of government funds and ensure that only priority and necessary expenses are  incurred.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  direct the  Municipal  Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that   only expenditures/ projects enumerated under the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1 are considered in the charging against the 20% Development Fund and  strictly utilized the fund in accordance with the guidelines set forth in the JMC  to fully achieve the intention and purpose for which it was established. Restore to the 20% Development Fund the amount of P1,262,364.73 improperly charged to the same fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Engineer to submit justification/basis on the computation of ABC at 12% for evaluation of the Audit Team.  We also recommend that  Section 22 of Revenue Regulation  No. 4-2007 issued by the Bureau of Internal Revenue be adopted  in the preparation of Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) to the advantage of the Municipal Government.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor strengthen the existing GDFPS by setting-up its structure/composition (i.e. Execom; TWG; Secretariat) as provided under Section 4.1.A of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA  JMC No. 2013-1 and define the roles and responsibilities of  GDFPS members  in accordance with the provisions of the JMC.
We also recommended that the GAD database, through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator Office, with the assistance of the GFPS,  be completed  and maintained/updated, in conformity with the data requirements of the JMC  to ensure that the Municipality’s GAD formulated policies, programs, plans and activities are gender-responsive and address gender issues and concerns within the Municipality.

We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the Inventory        Committee  to    complete the     physical count of all PPEs  owned  by the Municipality     and require the    Municipal Accountant and Municipal      Treasurer  to reconcile their respective PPE records and balances.

We also          recommended that the Municipal        Mayor advise  the Inventory        Committee and Municipal        Accountant  to reclassify to    inventory items  property with    serviceable life of more than one year but small enough to be considered as PPE, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2005-002 dated  April 4, 2005.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting and Treasury Offices have continuously updating and reconciling previous years PPE. However, for CY 2016 no actual physical count was conducted. The Municipal Treasurer based RPCPPE the ARE issued and records provided by the Assessor’s Office such as Tax Declarations of land, roads and buildings.

Inventory items such as calculators were dropped from the RPCPPE and corresponding ICS were issued to persons in possession of the items.
observations.reason: Prior Year’s record cannot be retrieved due to washedout records and documents such as voucher stored at the Municipal Gym in Galicia.  The Municipality is still seeking records relative to booking of PPEs to reconcile the amounts booked up and the RPCPPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended  that the         Municipal Mayor  instruct the        Municipal        Accountant to    provide          depreciation     expenses on the aforesaid property for fair presentation of the PPE, Government Equity and Depreciation accounts.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2016 depreciation has been provided for the market. The Accounting Office is still in the process of retrieving records to determine the dates of acquisition of other PPE not provided with depreciation.
observations.reason: Unavailability of records from which to base the computation of depreciation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the        Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant  to book up the cost of the ambulance amounting to P866,000.00 and the corresponding accumulated depreciation  in the General Fund book, as follows:
Debit            Motor Vehicles
Credit           Government Equity

Debit            Prior Year’s Adjustments
Credit           Accum. Dep.
                 -Motor Vehicles

We further       recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to include the Ambulance Van in the RPCPPE.
observations.managementAction: The donated ambulance had been booked-up by the Accountant and included in the RPCPPE by the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  advise the       Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer  to reconcile their respective records to come up with an  accurate and reliable balances of RPT and SET Receivable accounts and to support the accounts with subsidiary records based on the certified list of taxpayers submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.

We also          recommended that the  Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the penalty for late payment of taxes amounting to P12,228,661.76, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2016,  the difference between the accounting records and the Certified List of RPT Delinquencies prepared by the Treasurer’s Office totalled P9,914,772.97 or lower by P9,166,407.34 or 48.04%.  Penalty was recorded under JEV Nos. 13 and 14 for GF and SEF.
observations.reason: The Accountant is in the process of putting up database on taxpayers in the Municipality for easy monitoring and reconciliation of records with that of the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Treasurer to enforce          collection of the delinquent real property taxes by conducting tax campaign and granting incentives or relief to taxpayers thru compromise agreement,  with the authority of the Sangguniang Bayan, otherwise, avail of the remedies provided under Sections 256 to 260 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Extensive tax campaign is continuously implemented to improve collections.
observations.reason: Management believed that imposition of remedies provided under Sections 256 and 260 is under the jurisdiction of the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of the Province of Cavite.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation   that the         Municipal Mayor  instruct the       Municipal Accountant to analyze the  discrepancies in reporting the balances of RPT and SEF receivables and to take up the necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has made the adjustment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Accountant to revert  the Other Payables amounting to P4,925.00 which has been outstanding  for more than two years and against which no actual claims has been filed nor supported with valid documents to the unappropriated surplus, in line with the Commission on Audit Circular No. 76-45.
observations.managementAction: Other payable amounting to P4,925.00 was reverted back to unappropriated surplus under JEV No. 15 dated June 23, 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to remit immediately the amount of P164,916.40 to the National Treasury in compliance with the Supreme Court Decision on PDAF and COA Circular No. 94-013.
observations.managementAction: The balance of PDAF was remitted to the Bureau of Treasury on June 14, 2016.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds. They should limit the expenditures within the total income realized during the year to avoid incurrence of cash overdraft. They should likewise find ways to address the cash deficit.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2016 the cash deficit was reduced to P4,135,143.77 which correspond to the cash back up of the unexpended balance of 20% Development Fund and Trust Liability-DRRMF amounting to P1,689,282.23 and P2,445,861.54, respectively.
observations.reason: Income/collections were still not enough to cover the operational requirements of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Budget Officer  to require each end-user units of the Municipality to prepare their respective PPMP to support the budget for their different programs, activities and projects (PAPs).

We also recommended that the  BAC Secretariat review and consolidate the PPMPs  into an APP to be approved by the HOPE before any procurement of goods, services, and civil works is undertaken as required under Section 7.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Each unit/office of the Municipality was required to prepare its PPMP. The PPMPs were consolidated into an APP by the BAC Secretariat.

observations.recommendation: We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  require the       Municipal Accountant  to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to verify the discrepancy in recording the accounts Due to and Due from LGU of the Municipality with that of its reciprocal accounts in the books of the Province and make the necessary adjustments. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should request the Provincial Accountant to furnish him with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers showing the amount collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve as  basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality as provided under Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: Year-end reconciliation of accounts was not done, hence, different ending balances of the Due to and Due from for both the Municipality and the Province. The Municipal Accountant failed to follow-up the request.
observations.reason:
No meeting was held between the LGU and the Province.

The Province did not furnish the Municipal Accountant of copies of JEV.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  instruct the      Municipal Accountant to use the appropriate  account provided under the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units for Local Government Units to record the municipal transactions .

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor  instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to observe the proper classification and recording of expenses in the Appropriation and Obligation Registries.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Accountant to use the appropriate  account in accounting for expenses for meals/snacks and food items using the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.  .

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Budget Officer to observe the proper classification and recording of expenses in the registries and ensure that expenses incurred do not exceed their respective budget.  Likewise, we recommended  that the cost of meals and snacks served during meetings  of the respective offices of Municipality’s officials be charged against the representation allowance of the concerned officials, the purpose for which said allowance was granted.

observations.recommendation: .We  recommended  that the         Municipal Mayor fully comply with the  basic requirements of RA No. 9003, or the Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 to ensure protection of public health and the environment.
observations.managementAction: Strict implementation of waste segregation is being carried out and collection of residual waste is done on a regular basis.  The controlled dumpsite was permanently closed.
observations.reason: MRF could not be established in every barangay due to lack of space.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor ensure that written policies and guidelines on the procurement of JO services is formulated to categorically establish the rights and obligations of the Municipality and JO personnel and minimize the risk of applying different rules and procedures on JO personnel.

We also recommended that the hiring of JOs be covered with contract of service,  MOA or Job Order  in conformity with CSC Resolution No.  02-0790     dated June 5, 2002.
observations.managementAction: A written policy was formulated that will serve as guide in the procurement of JO services.  The hiring of JO personnel  are now covered with service contract.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor direct the       Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairman to submit the required documents to establish the validity, regularity and propriety of the transactions and the payments made.  Submission of the required documents will also facilitate the complete technical evaluation of the contracts, including evaluation of the reported accomplishments and final inspection of the projects.

We also recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims/ disbursements are properly and completely documented in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: The documents were submitted to the Audit Team which was forwarded to the COA-TAS.  Submission of complete documents were required before processing of claims.

observations.recommendation: We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  direct the       Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee or a surety bond callable upon demand, issued by a surety or insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance Commission and confirmed by the Municipality, are submitted by the contractor before payment of the 15% mobilization fee.
observations.managementAction: No claim for mobilization was processed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended   that the         Municipal Mayor  instruct the       Municipal Accountant  to deduct the ten percent (10%) retention  on the total amount due the contractor,  prior to any deduction, from every progress billing made or 10% of  the 50% of contract cost from final payment if no progress billings were made, in conformity with Section 6, Annex E of the Revised IRR of  RA 9184 to protect the interest of the government.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office is now deducting 10% retention on contracts entered into by the Municipality or require the posting of warranty security in lieu of cash.
